narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've shared your undergraduate GPA of 2.50, an LSAT score of 22, and your strong intention to pursue full-time studies, along with your middle-income family background. The admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on patterns from thousands of past students, estimated an 85.9% chance that you would fail the bar exam— a risk level that led to your application's rejection to protect the school's outcomes. This prediction hinged most heavily on your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, which placed you in the bottom 10% of your peers that year and strongly signaled to the model a higher likelihood of bar failure compared to typical students averaging around the middle of their classes. Your LSAT score was the second-biggest factor pushing the prediction toward failure, as it fell well below the dataset average of about 37, standing out as a major hurdle in how the model assesses readiness for the rigors ahead. Even your Year 1 decile of 1, while lower in overall influence, added to this by showing early academic struggles relative to peers who typically rank higher. These three elements—your later-year grades, test score, and early performance—dominated the decision, outweighing positives like your full-time commitment, which aligns with most successful students. In contrast, students who pass the bar on their first try usually have deciles in the mid-range, LSATs closer to 37, and stronger early showings, putting your profile notably below those benchmarks. While factors like your family income didn't sway things much here, the model saw your academics as the core mismatch. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background—many from similar Hispanic and middle-income families succeed with targeted improvements. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score toward the average, strengthening your recent grades through additional coursework or prep programs, and gathering strong recommendation letters highlighting your full-time dedication. Reapplying after addressing these could shift the model's view dramatically, opening doors at schools that value your unique path. Keep pushing; your determination as a full-time aspiring lawyer is already a strength worth building on.
